input_contract.format: Line 1: x q. Next q lines: operation type and parameter.

fields.name: operations
fields.description: Sequence of q operations. Each is either '1 a_i' (add team) or '2 K' (query cover).

constraints.team_ids: 1-indexed, assigned sequentially in order of arrival.

output_contract.format: For each type 2 query, output either a YES block or a NO block.

validity_rules: s % x == a_id % x OR s % x == (a_id + 1) % x.
Each team id is used at most once.
Each slot s in [0, K-1] is assigned exactly once.
Number of currently arrived teams whose valid slots intersect [L, R] is strictly less than R - L + 1.
[L, R] must be lexicographically smallest among all violating intervals (minimize L first, then R).

oracle_limits.rationale: Brute-force bipartite matching or exhaustive Hall subset enumeration is only feasible for very small scales due to K up to 1e9 and combinatorial explosion of interval checks.

test_buckets.name: basic_small
test_buckets.purpose: Verify basic YES/NO branching, output formatting, and simple assignment validity.
test_buckets.scale_intent: x ≤ 5, q ≤ 10, K ≤ 5, minimal team counts.

test_buckets.name: boundary_values
test_buckets.purpose: Test edge cases: K=1, K equals total teams, x=1, a_i=0, large a_i, immediate conflicts.

test_buckets.name: random_mixed
test_buckets.purpose: Stress test interleaved additions and queries with random parameters to catch state management bugs.
test_buckets.scale_intent: x ≤ 100, q ≤ 1000, K uniformly random in [1, 200], mixed YES/NO outcomes.

test_buckets.name: adversarial_hall
test_buckets.purpose: Force tight Hall violations to verify lexicographical minimality of [L, R] extraction and interval intersection logic.
test_buckets.scale_intent: x ≤ 50, q ≤ 500, carefully crafted a_i sequences to create minimal deficit intervals at specific L, R.

test_buckets.name: performance_large
test_buckets.purpose: Validate time/memory efficiency under maximum constraints and large K values.
test_buckets.scale_intent: x, q = 400000, K up to 1e9, sum of K for YES ≤ 400000, tests O(q log x) or similar complexity.

sample_tests.purpose: Demonstrates basic YES assignment and NO conflict interval output with x=3.
Demonstrates lexicographically smallest conflict interval selection when multiple violations exist.

performance_limits.expected_algorithmic_complexity: O(q log x) or O(q log q) per test case, leveraging segment tree or balanced BST for prefix surplus maintenance.

ambiguity_notes: K can reach 1e9, making direct interval enumeration or brute-force Hall checking impossible for large cases. Oracle is strictly limited to small scales (K ≤ 15).

The revision context reports standard_oracle_mismatch and checker_rejects_standard_output on random_mixed and performance_large tests. This spec isolates the contract to strictly verifiable rules (modulo constraints, slot uniqueness, Hall deficit count, lexicographical minimality) so the checker can be implemented independently of the standard solver's internal state.

Lexicographical minimality for NO cases requires the checker to independently verify that no interval [L', R'] with L' < L or (L' = L and R' < R) violates the Hall condition. This is computationally heavy for large K, so the checker should rely on the solver's output and verify it against a precomputed or efficiently queried deficit structure, or limit full minimality checks to small-scale tests.
The exact tie-breaking for YES assignments is explicitly 'any valid', so the checker must not enforce a specific matching order.
